coredata.dc:description: The use of nitrogen (N) fertilizers increases crop yield, but the accumulation of residual N in agricultural soils poses significant environmental risks. Improving the N use efficiency (NUE) of crops can help reduce N pollution. While nanomaterials have been shown to enhance crop agronomic traits, more research is needed to clarify the regulatory mechanisms involved. In this study, foliar spraying of carbon dots (CDs, 1 mg mL−1) derived from Salvia miltiorrhiza increased the activity of plasma membrane H+-ATPase in Arabidopsis thaliana roots, promoting the uptake, transport, and assimilation of NO3 − and NH4 +. The upregulation of N metabolism-related genes, such as AtAMTs and AtNRTs, was also observed in A. thaliana roots. Transcriptome analysis suggested that this regulatory effect is mediated by the shoot-to-root mobile polypeptide CEPD1 (C-terminally encoded peptide DOWNSTREAM 1) signaling pathway. Additionally, foliar application of CDs increased the NUE of sweetpotato (Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam.) from 2.5% to 8.1%. The upregulation of genes such as CEPD1 in leaves was observed following CDs application under different N conditions. Finally, foliar spraying of CDs significantly increased field yield and enhanced tolerance to low N stress in sweetpotato. Overall, this study demonstrated that foliar application of CDs improved NUE in plants through CEPD1-dependent signaling.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Material characterization of S. miltiorrhiza-derived CDs. HR-TEM image of the CDs. b Size distribution of the CDs. c FTIR spectrum of the CDs. d Full-scan XPS spectrum of the CDs. e High-resolution XPS O 1 s spectra of the CDs. f High-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of the CDs.

Foliar spraying of CDs increased the N uptake and assimilation of A. thaliana. a NH4 +, NO3 − and H+ ion fluxes in roots under normal N nutrient conditions with and without spraying of CDs. b Soil-cultivated growth phenotypes. c Fresh weight, leaf size, and seed yield (each point represents the yield of four plants). d N metabolism-related enzyme activities, including those of GOGAT, GS, and NR. e Seed NO3 −-N, and NH4 +-N contents. Each point represents one replicate. The labels “∗,” “∗∗,” “∗∗∗,” and “∗∗∗∗” indicate significant differences at p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001, and p < 0.0001, respectively. “ns” indicates no significant difference.

Foliar spraying of CDs did not improve the efficiency of N metabolism in cepd1 mutants. a NH4 +, NO3 −, and H+ ion fluxes in cepd1 mutant roots under normal N nutritional status with and without spraying of CDs. b Soil-cultivated growth phenotypes of the cepd1 mutant. c Fresh weight and leaf size of cepd1 mutants. d N metabolism-related enzyme activities of the cepd1 mutants, including: GOGAT, GS, and NR. Statistical significance is indicated by asterisks (∗, ∗∗, ∗∗∗, ∗∗∗∗) denoting p-values of less than 0.05, 0.01, 0.001, and 0.0001, respectively, whereas “ns” denotes no significant difference.

Analysis of differentially expressed genes in wild-type A. thaliana and cepd1 mutant roots after foliar spraying with CDs (A: WT, B: WT-CDs, C: cepd1-CDs). a Volcano map of DEGs (B VS A; C VS B). The red dots represent upregulated genes, the blue dots represent downregulated genes, and the gray dots represent genes that were not differentially expressed (P < 0.05, |logFC|>1.0). b Volcano map with gene labeling. The labeled genes are all associated with N metabolism. Gray represents nonsignificant genes, green metabolism Log2FC significant genes, blue represents P value significant genes, and red represents genes whose P value and Log2FC are significant. c Gene Ontology (GO) (biological process, cellular component, and molecular function) and KEGG enrichment analyses of A. thaliana roots after the application of CDs compared with those of the wild type. The vertical axis is the term, and the horizontal axis is the transformed FDR (-log10PValue).

Effects of foliar spraying of CDs on NO3 −/NH4 +/H+ transporter and N assimilation related genes. a Heatmap showing DEGs related to NO3 −/NH4 +/H+ transporters and N assimilation related to CDs-sprayed roots. The color scale and labeled numbers represent log2fold changes. b Effects of foliar spraying of CDs on the expression of genes related to wild-type A. thaliana and cepd1 mutant were examined via quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR). The labels “∗”, “∗∗”, “∗∗∗”, and “∗∗∗∗” denote the effects at p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001 and p < 0.0001, respectively. “ns” indicates no significant difference.

Effects of foliar spraying of CDs on H+/NH4 +/NO3 − steady-state ion fluxes in sweetpotato roots under different N conditions. The labels “∗”, “∗∗”, “∗∗∗”, and “∗∗∗∗” denote the effects at p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001 and p < 0.0001, respectively. “ns” indicates no significant difference.

Foliar spraying of CDs enhanced photosynthesis, N metabolism levels and field yield in sweetpotato. a Growth phenotypes of sweetpotato sprayed with and without CDs under different N conditions (35 d of growth). b Measurements of photosynthesis-related indices, including the net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, transpiration rate and chlorophyll content. c Growth-related indicators, including: root and aboveground fresh weights, root length and total N content. d NUE of sweetpotato in laboratory hydroponics experiments. e Field yield and aboveground fresh weight under normal nutrient conditions. f Field yield and aboveground fresh weight under NPK-deficient conditions. The labels “∗”, “∗∗”, “∗∗∗”, and “∗∗∗∗” denote the effects at p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001 and p < 0.0001, respectively. “ns” indicates no significant difference.
Foliar spraying of CDs to increase nitrogen metabolism in plants: a model diagram.

head.ce:title: Foliar application of carbon dots enhances nitrogen uptake and assimilation through CEPD1-dependent signaling in plants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of nitrogen (N) fertilizers increases crop yield, but the accumulation of residual N in agricultural soils poses significant environmental risks. Improving the N use efficiency (NUE) of crops can help reduce N pollution. While nanomaterials have been shown to enhance crop agronomic traits, more research is needed to clarify the regulatory mechanisms involved. In this study, foliar spraying of carbon dots (CDs, 1 mg mL−1) derived from Salvia miltiorrhiza increased the activity of plasma membrane H+-ATPase in Arabidopsis thaliana roots, promoting the uptake, transport, and assimilation of NO3 − and NH4 +. The upregulation of N metabolism-related genes, such as AtAMTs and AtNRTs, was also observed in A. thaliana roots. Transcriptome analysis suggested that this regulatory effect is mediated by the shoot-to-root mobile polypeptide CEPD1 (C-terminally encoded peptide DOWNSTREAM 1) signaling pathway. Additionally, foliar application of CDs increased the NUE of sweetpotato (Ipomoea batatas (L.) Lam.) from 2.5% to 8.1%. The upregulation of genes such as CEPD1 in leaves was observed following CDs application under different N conditions. Finally, foliar spraying of CDs significantly increased field yield and enhanced tolerance to low N stress in sweetpotato. Overall, this study demonstrated that foliar application of CDs improved NUE in plants through CEPD1-dependent signaling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen (N) is essential for the growth, development, and yield of plants and functions as a key element in the biosynthesis of proteins, chlorophyll, amino acids, nucleic acids, and certain secondary metabolites (Mu and Chen, 2021). The application of N fertilizers in agricultural practices is a critical strategy for increasing crop productivity. The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and International Fertilizer Association (IFA) project that the global consumption of N fertilizer will grow by 1.4% annually from 2023 to 2027, driven by rising agricultural and food production needs. However, the excessive use of N fertilizers does not consistently increase crop productivity, largely because of the low nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) of plants (NUE <50%) (Govindasamy et al., 2023). Additionally, the overapplication of N fertilizers can lead to excessive vegetative growth and delay the maturation of crops, ultimately resulting in negative impacts on overall crop yields. The runoff of N from agricultural fields significantly contributes to global N pollution, thereby causing serious environmental issues, including soil acidification and water eutrophication (Xuejun et al., 2022). Therefore, improving the utilization efficiency of N fertilizers is vital for optimizing crop yield and quality while simultaneously reducing application rates and mitigating associated environmental pollution.

To achieve these goals, comprehensive and effective management strategies for crops and land must be implemented. Gu and colleagues identified 11 management measures, including modifications to N fertilizer application, adherence to the 4R principles, and enhancements in irrigation and tillage practices (Gu et al., 2023). However, the implementation of these measures has faced obstacles such as cost and complexity. Therefore, it is crucial to explore more feasible approaches to strengthen N pollution management, thereby fostering the harmonious development of future low-N ecological agriculture.

Nanotechnology is recognized as a significant area of scientific advancement in the 21st century (Aleixandre-Tudó et al., 2020). The application of nanotechnology in agriculture is still in its infancy, offering both opportunities and challenges. The integration of nanomaterials into agricultural processes, particularly for enhancing plant growth, fertilizer efficiency, and resilience against abiotic stresses, has attracted considerable interest in recent years (Faizan et al., 2021; Rai-Kalal and Jajoo, 2021; Zulfiqar and Ashraf, 2021). Emerging research suggests that the use of nanomaterials can increase N utilization in crops and promote plant growth. For example, cerium dioxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) have shown promise in alleviating low N (LN) and high N (HN) stress in hydroponically cultivated rice plants (Wang et al., 2020). Additionally, multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have been demonstrated to improve plant growth by regulating key enzymes involved in carbon and N metabolism, leading to increased carbohydrate yield, enhanced N utilization efficiency, and overall growth (Hu et al., 2021). The application of multiwalled carbon nanotubes in soil significantly enhances N metabolism in rapeseed, resulting in an 85.9% improvement in NUE (Cai et al., 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs) are essential components of carbon-based nanomaterials that hold significant promise for agriculture owing to their water solubility, optical properties, low toxicity, and small size (Milenković et al., 2021). Research indicates that CDs enhance light harvesting, electron transfer, and photosynthesis in plants while also improving their adaptability to various environments by facilitating Ca2+ migration (Li et al., 2022). Additionally, the foliar application of CDs has been shown to increase the uptake of iron and other nutrients in wheat (Zhu et al., 2023). Under drought stressed conditions, the root application of CDs can increase soybean Nase activity by 8.6% and increase the N content in both the aboveground and root parts of soybean plants by 18.5% and 14.8%, respectively (Wang et al., 2022). Similarly, foliar spraying of CDs during drought stress enhances soybean photosynthesis and amino acid biosynthesis, thus promoting growth and N metabolism in soybeans (Ji et al., 2023). Compared with other carbon nanomaterials, CDs synthesized from biomass have the added advantage of being widely available and less toxic (Wareing et al., 2021). Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms underlying CDs-plant interactions is crucial for their environmentally friendly use and for promoting sustainable, low-carbon practices in agriculture.

Nitrate N and ammonium N are the primary N sources for plants, with nitrate N being the predominant form utilized by terrestrial plants, despite its uneven distribution in soil. Over evolutionary time, plants have developed a sophisticated signaling network to respond effectively to external N conditions and to coordinate internal N demands, ensuring an adequate supply of N across different environments (Hui et al., 2022). For example, in Arabidopsis thaliana (A. thaliana), CEPD1 and CEPD2 function as shoot‒root mobile polypeptides involved in the systemic regulation of N acquisition (Ohkubo et al., 2017). However, the potential role of externally applied CDs in modulating this regulatory network to increase plant NUE warrants further investigation. This study employed the model plant A. thaliana and the important crop sweetpotato to explore: (1) the effects of foliar application of CDs on the N uptake patterns in A. thaliana roots and the activity of plasma membrane H+-ATPase under different N conditions; (2) the potential relationship between CDs-mediated leaf-stem-root signal transduction and the shoot-root system peptide CEPD1; (3) the influence of CDs on N uptake, transport, and assimilation in A. thaliana roots; and (4) the implications of foliar carbon points in agricultural production concerning NUE, yield, and quality of sweetpotato.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized following a predetermined protocol (Li et al., 2022). In summary, 3.5 g of S. miltiorrhiza powder (containing 10% hydrochloric acid B) was added to 70 mL of deionized water, transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated to 150 °C for a duration of 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove any remaining particulate matter. The CDs were subsequently dialyzed for 6 h using a 3500 Da dialysis bag. For foliar spraying experiments on plants, the concentrated solution of CDs was diluted to approximately 1 mg mL−1 on the basis of previously reported optimal spraying concentrations. The morphology of the CDs was examined with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope, while the composition and surface functional groups were analyzed via a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and a Nicolet IS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant materials, growth conditions, and treatments
ce:section.ce:para: The sweetpotato (variety Xuzishu 8) and the model plant A. thaliana were selected as experimental subjects. Uniform sweetpotato seedlings, each 15 cm in length, were hydroponically cultured in ultrapure water to maintain consistent nutritional conditions. After three days, seedlings with similar growth conditions were transferred to 1/4 Hoagland solution for continued cultivation, with the nutrient solution being replaced every three days. Following seven days of acclimatization, the seedlings were subjected to different N treatments. All the seedlings were maintained in an environment with a temperature of 28 °C, a photoperiod of 16 h, and a relative humidity of 60%. For the A. thaliana experiments, seeds of the wild type (ecotype Colombia-0 (Col-0)) and cepd1 were surface sterilized via 2% (v/v) sodium hypochlorite for 1 min, followed by thorough rinsing with sterile water. The sterilized seeds were then placed on Murashige and Skoog (MS) 3% sucrose and 0.8% agar and incubated in the dark at 4 °C for three days. The seedlings were subsequently transferred to a growth chamber maintained at 22 °C, with a light intensity of 120 μmol m−2s−1, and a photoperiod of 16 h light/8 h dark. Seven-day-old A. thaliana seedlings were then hydroponically or soil-cultured for physiological data measurements or transcriptome sequencing, respectively.

Sweetpotato (variety Xuzishu 8) and A. thaliana seedlings were cultivated in 1/4 Hoagland solution with varying N concentrations. Two nitrogen concentrations were used in this experiment: 1) The normal N treatments included 4.5 mM N (composed of 4 mM KNO3 and 0.5 mM NH4NO3); 2) 0.3 mM N (0.25 mM KNO3 and 0.05 mM NH4NO3) in the low N treatment. Each treatment group included a leaf spray CDs group and a control group. For sweetpotato, a 1 mg mL−1 CDs solution was applied to the leaves every two days for a total of three applications, followed by weekly sprays. Measurements of root ion fluxes were conducted two posttreatment, photosynthetic indices were analyzed at 25 days, and physiological indices including fresh weight, dry weight, stem length, root length, leaf surface area, N content and N use efficiency difference (NUEdiff), were assessed at 35 days. NUEdiff = [(N uptake of crop with N application - N uptake of the crop without N application)/Fertilizer N input] × 100% (Quan et al., 2021). For A. thaliana, a similar 1 mg mL−1 CDs solution was sprayed on the leaves every two days, and root samples were collected for ion flux measurements after 7 days.

The net fluxes of H+, NH4 +, and NO3 − were quantified via an NMT system (NMT-100SIM-YG, Younger USA LLC, Amherst, MA). Ion-selective microelectrodes (with a tip diameter of 2–4 μm) were fabricated according to established protocols, and each microelectrode was calibrated prior to measuring the ion flux (Yu et al., 2020). The calibration solutions for NO3 − consisted of concentrations of 0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, and 1 mM; for NH4 +, concentrations of 0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, and 1 mM were prepared; and for H+, corresponding to pH values of 5, 6, and 7 were utilized. The Nernstian slope of the ion-selective microelectrodes for H+, NH4 +, and NO3 − exceeded 52 mV/decade (or < −52 mV/decade), and the ion flux was calculated via established methods.

Steady-state flux measurements of H+, NH4 +, and NO3 −: After 14 days of normal and low N treatment, sweetpotato root tips (approx. 3 cm) were taken for the ion flux assay. These root segments were immersed in test solutions prepared with 1/4 Hoagland solution (N concentrations of 4.5 mM or 0.3 mM, pH = 5.7). Flux measurements of H+, NH4 +, and NO3 − were recorded in three different regions of the root tip: the meristematic zone (0–1 mm from the apex), the elongation zone (1–3 mm from the apex), and the mature zone (5–10 mm from the apex). Continuous recordings were taken for 4–5 min at each measurement point within these zones. Similar measurements were conducted on A. thaliana after 7 days of treatment, using approximately 2 cm root segments from both A. thaliana (Col-0, cepd1) lines. Measurements were taken in the meristematic zone (0–0.5 mm from the apex), elongation zone (1–1.5 mm from the apex), and mature zone (5–10 mm from the apex).

The net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, intracellular carbon dioxide concentration, and transpiration rate of sweet potato seedlings subjected to low N stress were assessed via a portable photosynthesis system (LI-6400, LI-COR, Lincoln, NE) after 25 days of treatment. The intracellular carbon dioxide concentration was maintained at a constant level of 400 μmol-mol−1, with the photosynthetic photon flux density set at 1000 μmol-m−2-s−1. The chlorophyll content was evaluated via a portable chlorophyll meter.

In the initial field experiment, 4∗35 seedlings were transplanted into both control fields and low-N (KNP) long-term localized fields. The field site was located at the Xuzhou Institute of Agricultural Sciences, Jiangsu, China (Ministry of Agriculture/National Agricultural Experimental Station for Soil Quality), and the nutrient content data are shown in Fig. S5. After 4 weeks of growth, the plants were categorized into four groups, each receiving weekly foliar spray treatments of either water or a 1 mg mL−1 CDs solution for 5 weeks. In the A. thaliana experiment, following a 7-day soil cultivation period, the plants were divided into two groups: one receiving water treatment and the other receiving CDs spray treatment every three days for 3 weeks, during which physiological data were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of glutamine synthase (GS) activity, glutamine oxoglutarate amino transferase (GOGAT) activity, nitrate reductase (NR) activity and N content
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh root samples weighing 0.4 g were rapidly frozen in liquid N and homogenized in 5 mL of 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). The homogenate was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the resulting supernatant was stored for subsequent analyses of GS activity, GOGAT activity, and NR activity. Enzyme activities were quantified via specialized assay kits from Jiangsu Jianye Biotechnology Research Institute (Nanjing, China). Sweetpotato samples, including the normal N-control, normal N-CDs, low N-control, and low N-CDs samples, were subjected to thermal treatment at 108 °C for 30 min, followed by dehydration at 80 °C for 24 h in an oven. Total N determination was performed via the sodium salicylate Kjeldahl method, with measurements carried out via the AMS Smartchem200 fully automated intermittent chemical analyzer. For the A. thaliana experiment, fresh seeds (1 g) were immersed in extraction solution, homogenized under low-temperature conditions, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected for subsequent determination of NH4 +-N or NO3 −-N contents. NH4 +-N and NO3 −-N contents were quantified via specialized assay kits procured from Suzhou Geruisi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA sequencing and analysis of transcript levels
ce:section.ce:para: After a 3-week soil incubation period (A. thaliana: Col-0, cepd1), root samples from both control and CDs-treated plants were collected for transcriptome analysis. Total RNA was extracted via the Shengong Total RNA Purification Kit (Shenggong Biotech Shanghai Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The subsequent steps included assessing RNA purity, quantification, and integrity in accordance with established protocols. The construction of cDNA libraries and sequencing on the Illumina HiSeq 2500 platform were conducted at Shengong Biotech (Shanghai, China). Following a quality assessment of the raw sequencing data, Trimmomatic was used to remove low-quality sequences. The cleaned sequences were then aligned with the reference genome (TAIR10) via HISAT2, and the alignment results were analyzed via RSeQC. Data pertaining to A. thaliana leaves following CDs spray treatment were obtained from a previously published study (Fig. S1) (Zhu et al., 2023).

Total RNA was extracted from the root samples collected from various experimental series via the TRIzol reagent (Takara Bio Inc.). Quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) was conducted following established protocols (Pan et al., 2024). The primers used were specifically designed to target genes from sweetpotato and A. thaliana (Tables S1 and S2). Relative gene expression levels were normalized to the reference gene (IbUBI or AtActin) and calculated via the 2^−ΔΔCt method.

Statistical analyses were conducted via GraphPad Prism software. The data were analyzed via analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significance levels are denoted as follows: “∗”, “∗∗”, “∗∗∗”, and “∗∗∗∗” represent significant differences at P < 0.05, P < 0.01, P < 0.001, and P < 0.0001, respectively. The error bars were calculated using the standard error of the mean (SEM).

CDs derived from S. miltiorrhiza were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method (Li et al., 2022). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed spherical particles with a size distribution ranging from 2 to 7 nm (Fig. 1 a), with an average size of 4.30 nm (Fig. 1b). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) confirmed the presence of functional groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 1c). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis indicated that the CDs primarily consisted of C and O (Fig. 1d). High-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra confirmed the presence of C=C, C=O, C-OH, and C-O in the CDs (Fig. 1e and f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The activation of PM H+-ATPase activity by foliar-applied CDs led to a modification in the pattern of N uptake in A. thaliana roots
ce:section.ce:para: The application of foliar sprays of CDs has induced changes in the nutrient ion transport pattern within wheat roots. In this study, the noninvasive microtest technique (NMT) was employed to evaluate N ion transport in the roots of A. thaliana. The findings indicate that following the foliar application of CDs, there was a significant increase in the efficiency of NH4 + uptake in both the meristematic and elongation zones of A. thaliana roots, with increases of 2.1-fold and 2.7-fold, respectively, after 7 days (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, foliar application also increased NO3 − uptake in the meristematic zone. Under low N stress, the uptake of NH4 + in all three regions of A. thaliana root tips significantly increased after the application of CDs compared with that of the controls (Fig. S2a). These results suggest that foliar spraying of CDs effectively alters the N uptake pattern in A. thaliana roots.

With the exception of leguminous plants, most terrestrial plants rely on root uptake to utilize inorganic N from the soil. During the uptake and assimilation of ammonium and nitrate, a significant amount of H+ or hydroxide ions are produced. For example, the assimilation of one NH4 + molecule requires the involvement of two H+ molecules (Masclaux-Daubresse et al., 2010). Changes in the H+ concentration can affect the cytoplasmic pH. Therefore, the uptake of N ions by roots necessitates the action of plasma membrane H⁺-ATPase, which provides the to maintain pH homeostasis (Zhu et al., 2009). Additionally, the uptake of nitrate and ammonium is driven by hydrogen ion concentration gradients or the proton motive force generated by the PM H+-ATPase (Quaggiotti et al., 2003). Following the foliar application of CDs, alterations in H+ transport were observed in all three regions of A. thaliana root tips. A decreased H+ influx was noted under normal N conditions (Fig. 2a), whereas strong H⁺ efflux activity was recorded under low N conditions (Fig. S2a). The increased H+ efflux activity facilitated the uptake of NH4 + and NO3 − from the roots. These findings suggest that the foliar application of CDs may modify the N uptake pattern in A. thaliana roots by increasing PM H+-ATPase activity, thereby increasing the uptake and assimilation of ammonium and nitrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foliar spraying of CDs promoted A. thaliana growth
ce:section.ce:para: Efficient N uptake and integration are key for plant development. An adequate N supply, combined with high NUE, supports growth. However, excessive unassimilated N in plant tissues can hinder growth. Plants typically assimilate N into amino acids via enzymes such as NR, nitrite reductase (NiR), GOGAT, and GS. These amino acids are then used in metabolism and protein synthesis, with the rest redistributed to storage organs, such as young leaves, roots, and developing seeds (Zhang et al., 2015).

In this study, A. thaliana showed improved growth after foliar application of CDs (Fig. 2b). This treatment increased A. thaliana fresh weight (shoots and roots), leaf surface area, and seed yield by 25%, 70%, 33%, and 34%, respectively (Fig. 2c). The levels of enzymes involved in N assimilation, including GS, GOGAT, and NR, also significantly increased in the roots (Fig. 2d). Foliar application of CDs increased NO3 −-N and NH4 +-N in the seeds by 51% and 14% (Fig. 2e), indicating improved N uptake in the root system. The increases in the activities of GS, GOGAT, NR, and other enzymes further enhanced N assimilation. Thus, foliar application of CDs improved N uptake and assimilation, promoting plant growth and seed quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foliar spraying of CDs does not promote growth or N metabolism in A. thaliana cepd1 mutants
ce:section.ce:para: Previous experiments have demonstrated that CDs improve N uptake and assimilation efficiency in the roots of A. thaliana. However, the mechanisms by which foliar-applied CDs regulate N transport in the root system remain to be further investigated. Research indicates that A. thaliana utilizes a systemic long-distance signaling mechanism to regulate N acquisition (Gansel et al., 2001; Ruffel et al., 2011). Owing to the heterogeneous distribution of nitrate in the soil, certain root tissues may experience N deficiency. This triggers a root-to-shoot signaling cascade mediated by CEP peptides to regulate N distribution (Ohyama et al., 2008; Tabata et al., 2014). Upstream CEP signals are detected in the phloem, leading to the production of specific peptides such as CEPD1, CEPD2, and CEPDL2 (Ota et al., 2020; Yoon et al., 2021). These peptides, derived from the stem, function as long-distance signaling molecules from shoot to root, regulating the expression of the nitrate transporter gene NRT2.1 (Yoon et al., 2021). Notably, our previous data indicated that the foliar application of CDs significantly increased the expression levels of CEP1, CEP receptor 1 (CEPR1), CEPR2, CEPD1, and CEPD2 (Fig. S1a). These findings suggest that CDs may increase root N assimilation by modulating this systemic long-distance signaling mechanism.

To further explore this regulatory relationship, the cepd1 mutant was selected as the experimental model for subsequent investigations. Foliar spraying of CDs did not induce any changes in the root N transport patterns of the cepd1 mutant, nor did it result in any increase in PM H+-ATPase activity (Fig. 3 a). The growth phenotypes of the cepd1 mutant were not significantly different between the control group and the CDs-treated group (Fig. 3b and c). Furthermore, there were no notable variations in the activities of GS, GOGAT, or NR (Fig. 3d). These findings suggest that the foliar spraying of CDs does not facilitate the growth of the cepd1 mutant through the enhancement of the N metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foliar spraying of CDs regulates N metabolism in A. thaliana by enhancing shoot to root polypeptide signaling
ce:section.ce:para: The lack of response observed in cepd1 mutants to CDs-mediated N regulation indicates that the signal transduction pathway from leaves to shoots to roots induced by the foliar application of CDs, may rely on the plant’s endogenous systemic long-distance signaling. However, this hypothesis requires further validation at the transcriptional level. Consequently, we performed root transcriptomic profiling on wild-type (WT) A. thaliana, wild-type A. thaliana treated with CDs (WT-CDs), and cepd1 mutants treated with CDs (cepd1-CDs) (Fig. S2c). Our findings indicate that, compared with the WT sample, the WT-CDs significantly downregulated the expression of 1407 genes and upregulated the expression of 888 genes (Fig. 4 a). Subsequent Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) analyses revealed that the upregulated pathways were associated primarily with transmembrane transport (Fig. 4c), which is consistent with the role of CDs in regulating ion transport in plant roots.

In contrast, cepd1-CDs presented a greater number of differentially expressed genes, with 743 genes downregulated and 1122 genes upregulated compared with those of the WT (Fig. S4a). Notably, these DEGs were also enriched in pathways related to ion transmembrane transport (Fig. S4b). Compared with WT-CDs, cepd1-CDs presented a lower number of differentially expressed genes, with 111 genes downregulated and 250 genes upregulated (Fig. 4a). An examination of selected genes associated with N metabolism revealed that, unlike those in the WT, the majority of these genes were upregulated in the roots of both WT-CDs and cepd1-CDs (Fig. 5 a and b). Nevertheless, the expression levels of these genes were lower in cepd1-CDs than in WT-CDs (Fig. 4b). The results obtained at the transcriptional level suggest that the application of foliar CDs enhances N metabolism in the roots of A. thaliana, with this increase being facilitated by systemic long-distance N regulatory signals mediated by CEPD1. Furthermore, a thorough investigation of these key genes is essential for elucidating the mechanisms underlying the regulation of plant N metabolism by foliar-applied CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foliar spraying of CDs affected the expression of genes related to N metabolism in A. thaliana roots
ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of N by plants includes absorption, transport, with numerous genes playing vital roles in regulating these processes. Plants exhibit selective absorption of N, a process mediated by transporter proteins located on the cell membrane that are responsible for transmembrane uptake. Two primary gene families, the nitrate transporter (NRT) family and the ammonium transporter (AMT) family, are critical for the transmembrane transport of NO3 − and NH4 +, respectively.

Currently, in A. thaliana, the NRT1 and NRT2 gene families have been isolated, comprising 53 and 7 members, respectively, each functioning at varying concentrations of nitrate (Tsay et al., 2007). Following the foliar application of CDs, we observed upregulation of differentially expressed nitrate genes, including NRT1.2, NRT1.7, NRT1.8, NRT2.1, and NRT2.2 (Fig. 5a). RT-qPCR analysis further confirmed the accuracy of these transcriptome data (Fig. 5b). Nitrate uptake and assimilation involve transportation and remobilization from the roots to the stems. RNT1.2 is responsible for mediating NO3 − uptake in the roots of A. thaliana (Pan et al., 2020). NRT2.1 and NRT2.2 are significantly upregulated in response to N deficiency signals and primarily regulate nitrate influx in A. thaliana roots. NRT1.7 is expressed in the endodermis and facilitates the remobilization of nitrate from source to sink (Fan et al., 2009). NRT1.8 mediates nitrate efflux from the roots and participates in loading and unloading processes within the root stele or stem vasculature (Li et al., 2010). Furthermore, WT-CDS roots were compared with cepd1-CD roots, the expression levels of NRT1.2, NRT1.7, RNT1.8, NRT2.1, and NRT2.2 were decreased in cepd1-CDs roots (Fig. 5a and b). Foliar spraying of CDs increased the expression of NRT1.2, NRT2.1, and NRT2.2, promoting. nitrate uptake in the roots. Elevated expression of NRT1.7 and NRT1.8 facilitates the upward transport and reutilization of nitrate, thereby improving nitrate utilization efficiency. Conversely, the absence of systemic long-distance transport signals diminishes the regulatory effect of CDs on root nitrate and suppresses the expression of relevant genes.

Studies on A. thaliana have identified 5 AMT1 (AMT1;1 to AMT1;5) and 1 AMT2;1 transport gene, each of which plays distinct roles under different conditions (Sohlenkamp et al., 2002). AMT1;1, AMT1;2, AMT1;3, and AMT1;5 are predominantly expressed in the roots of Arabidopsis and serve as the primary transport proteins for high-affinity ammonium uptake (Hao et al., 2020). Consistent with the nitrate-regulated genes, following the foliar application of CDs, the ammonium-regulated genes AMT1;1 and AMT2;1 presented increased expression in the roots of WT-CDS in comparison with the wild type (Fig. 5a). RT-qPCR analysis revealed that, relative to those in the wild type, the expression levels of AMT1;1 and AMT2;1 in the roots of WT-CDS were elevated 3-fold and 2-fold, respectively (Fig. 5b). Notably, the expression levels of AMT1;1 and AMT2;1 in the roots of WT-CDS were greater than those in cepd1-CDs (Fig. 5b). CDs regulate the ammonium uptake process by increasing the expression of AMT1;1 and AMT2;1, whereas a lack of CEPD1 signaling adversely affects this regulatory mechanism.

Plant roots absorb inorganic N from the soil, where it is transformed, transported, and assimilated within the body. This process involves energy conversion and the catalysis of key enzymes (Tang et al., 2022). An analysis of the DEGs revealed that the application of foliar CDs increased the expression of two homologous genes, NIA1 and NIA2, which encode nitrate reductases in A. thaliana (Fig. 5a). Compared with that in the WT, the expression of NIA1 and NIA2 in the roots of WT-CDs was upregulated 2.5-fold and 2-fold, respectively (Fig. 5b). Conversely, the expression levels of NIA1 and NIA2 in the roots of cepd1-CDs were slightly lower than those in the roots of WT-CDs (Fig. 5a and b). Furthermore, similar results were observed for NIR1, the gene responsible for nitrite reductase (Fig. 5a and b). Higher plants possess two forms of GOGAT, namely, ferredoxin-dependent glutamate synthase (Fd-GOGAT) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH)-dependent GOGAT, which are encoded by the genes GLU1, GLU2, and GLT1, respectively (Kissen et al., 2010; Krapp et al., 2014). Following the application of foliar CDs, both GLU2 and GLT1 in A. thaliana roots presented increased expression levels (Fig. 5a and b). Specifically, GLU2 and GLT1 presented 1.9-fold and 2.4-fold higher expression levels, respectively, than the wild type. These findings suggest that CEPD1 signaling plays a critical role in CDs-regulated nitrate N assimilation in plants.

Additionally, after the application of CDs, a significant increase in PM H+-ATPase activity was observed in A. thaliana roots, accompanied by substantial upregulation of the regulatory genes AHA1 and AHA2 (Fig. 5a and b). Increased PM H+-ATPase activity facilitates N metabolism in plant roots. For example, the overexpression of PM H+-ATPase 1 in rice promotes the uptake and assimilation of NH4 + in roots (Zhang et al., 2021). Compared with those in WT-CDs, AHA1 and AHA2 were significantly downregulated in cepd1-CDs (Fig. 5a and b). Foliar spraying of CDs also promoted the expression of the leaf CEDP1 gene (Fig. S1). As a mobile polypeptide, the transfer of CEDP1 from shoots to roots enhances N uptake in the roots. Concurrently, the plasma membrane H⁺-ATPase activity significantly increased following CDs spraying. However, the regulatory effect appears to be diminished in the absence of CEPD1. Importantly, there are currently no published reports indicating a correlation between CEPD1 signaling and PM H+-ATPase, which calls for further investigation. CEPD1 may increase PM H+-ATPase activity and, consequently, root N metabolism through unidentified pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Foliar spraying of CDs improved photosynthesis, low N stress tolerance and field yield of sweetpotato
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that foliar spraying of CDs improves N uptake and utilization in the model plant A. thaliana. However, further research is needed to evaluate the effectiveness of foliar spraying of CDs in improving NUE in crops. This study focused on sweetpotato, a strategically important root crop widely cultivated in China (Pan et al., 2024). Our findings indicate that foliar spraying of CDs affects ion transport in sweetpotato roots under various N conditions (Fig. 6 ). Additionally, the increased activity of PM H+-ATPase observed to promote N assimilation homeostasis in sweetpotato roots under salt stress (Yu et al., 2016). Under conditions of low N availability, a 7-day foliar application of CDs resulted in a significant increase in H+ efflux in the meristematic and elongation regions of sweetpotato roots compared with that in control group. This increase suggests an increase in PM H+-ATPase activity, thereby facilitating the absorption rates of nitrate and ammonium N (Fig. 6). Similarly, under normal N conditions, foliar spraying of CDs increased PM H+-ATPase activity in sweetpotato roots, significantly increased H+ efflux in the elongation zone, and resulted in increases of 1.5-fold and 2.1-fold increase in the uptake rates of nitrate ions in the meristematic and elongation zones, respectively (Fig. 6).

Twenty-five days after foliar spraying, we measured the net photosynthetic rate (Pn) of sweetpotato leaves in addition to other photosynthetic parameters, such as stomatal conductance (Gs), intracellular CO2 concentration (Ci), transpiration rate (Tr), and chlorophyll index (CCI) (Fig. 7 b; Fig. S3c). The results indicated that, under low N conditions, Pn, Gs, Tr, and CCI increased by 2.5-fold, 1.8-fold, 1.6-fold, and 1.8-fold, respectively, compared with those of the control (Fig. 7b). Under normal N conditions, with the exception of the CCI, these parameters also increased, with values increasing by 1.4-fold, 1.3-fold, and 1.4-fold, respectively (Fig. 7b). Thirty-five days into the growth phase, we assessed the growth indicators of the sweetpotato. Following the foliar spraying of CDs under varying N conditions, sweetpotato biomass (aboveground and underground parts), stem length, root length, and total N content in the tubers all increased (Fig. 7a and c; Fig. S3d and e). The total N content increased by 1.5 times and 1.4 times, respectively. Notably, laboratory hydroponics experiments demonstrated that spraying CDs significantly elevated the sweetpotato NUE from 2.5% to 8.1% (Fig. 7d).

To verify the potential of foliar spraying of CDs to increase crop N utilization in practical agricultural settings, we conducted field experiments in designated experimental fields (Fig. S5). The findings revealed that foliar spraying of CDs significantly increased yield under both nutrient-deficient and normal nutritional conditions, with increases of 1.26-fold and 1.3-fold respectively (Fig. 7e and f). In contrast to the results of the indoor physiological experiments, there was no increase in the aboveground fresh weight of the sweetpotato plants (Fig. 7e and f). Foliar spraying of CDs appeared to increase the N metabolism levels in sweetpotato roots, which may account for the observed increase in yield.

Furthermore, foliar spraying of CDs influenced gene expression in sweetpotato leaves and roots under different N conditions. The RT-qPCR data indicated that the expression of CEP5, CEPR1, CEPR2, CEPD1, and CEPD2 was upregulated in sweetpotato leaves following the foliar application of CDs (Fig. S6a). Additionally, genes regulated by N presented similar upregulation in sweet potato roots as that observed in A. thaliana (Fig. S6b). In conclusion, the application of foliar CDs resulted in increased N use efficiency, the leaf photosynthetic rate, and resilience to low nutrient conditions in sweetpotato by activating CEPD1-mediated signaling pathways, ultimately leading to increased sweetpotato yield.

CDs, newly discovered nanomaterials, hold significant potential for agricultural applications. The use of CDs has improved crop tolerance to abiotic stressors such as salinity and drought, as well as increased photosynthetic rates and growth levels. However, further research is necessary to fully elucidate the mechanisms by which CDs interact with plants to modulate relevant physiological responses. In this study, we observed that foliar spraying of CDs (1 mg mL−1) regulated N metabolism in both A. thaliana and sweetpotato roots. This regulatory effect necessitates the involvement of systemic long-distance signaling peptide CEPD1. On the one hand, foliar spraying of CDs enhances photosynthesis and the expression of systemic N-regulating peptides in leaves. On the other hand, it increases the activity of PM H+-ATPase in roots, which facilitates the uptake, transport, and assimilation of NO3 −and NH4 +. Ultimately, this process promotes N metabolism in both A. thaliana and sweetpotato, resulting in increased yields (Fig. 8 ). Foliar spraying of CDs has proven to be an effective method for enhancing agronomic traits in crops.
